$comment: Module 6 - Variational & Near-Term Algorithms. One item per objective. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views. JSON backslashes single-escaped.
title: Module 6 - Variational & Near-Term Algorithms

quizzes.description: Hybrid loop, ansatz & cost, optimizer, the variational bound.

questions.text: Which best describes a hybrid variational algorithm?
questions.hint: The loop alternates a quantum cost evaluation with a classical parameter update.
questions.explanation: Variational algorithms split work: the quantum device evaluates a cost (expectation value) for the current parameters, and a classical optimizer proposes better ones, iterating to convergence.

options.text: a quantum device prepares an ansatz and measures a cost, and a classical optimizer updates the parameters — repeated in a loop
a purely classical optimization with no quantum circuit
a single quantum circuit run once with no optimization
a quantum computer that trains itself with no classical help

questions.text: In $|\psi(\vec\theta)\rangle = U(\vec\theta)|0\rangle$, what are $U(\vec\theta)$ and the cost?
questions.hint: The ansatz is the tunable circuit; the cost is what you minimize.
questions.explanation: $U(\vec\theta)$ is the trainable ansatz preparing the trial state; the cost function is an expectation value (e.g. energy) estimated from measurements.

options.text: $U(\vec\theta)$ is the parameterized ansatz circuit; the cost is an expectation value measured on the device
$U(\vec\theta)$ is a measurement; the cost is the number of qubits
$U(\vec\theta)$ is the classical optimizer; the cost is the circuit depth
$U(\vec\theta)$ is a random unitary that is never changed

questions.text: Why is each optimizer iteration expensive on real hardware?
questions.hint: Shot noise means each cost value needs many repetitions.
questions.explanation: Estimating an expectation value to useful precision needs many shots ($\sim 1/\epsilon^2$), and gradient-based optimizers evaluate the cost multiple times per step — a major overhead.

options.text: each expectation value must be estimated from many shots, and gradient methods need several such evaluations
the classical optimizer is exponentially slow
the ansatz cannot be changed between iterations
measurement is free, so there is no cost

questions.text: For any Hamiltonian $H$ with ground energy $E_0$, every trial state obeys $\langle\psi(\vec\theta)|H|\psi(\vec\theta)\rangle \ge E_0$.
questions.explanation: True. The Rayleigh quotient is bounded below by the smallest eigenvalue, so the measured energy can never dip below $E_0$; equality holds only at the ground state.

options.text: True
False

quizzes.description: VQE goal, Pauli decomposition, measuring expectations, chemistry.

questions.hint: Minimize the energy expectation; the variational principle makes it an upper bound.
questions.explanation: VQE minimizes $E(\vec\theta)=\langle\psi(\vec\theta)|H|\psi(\vec\theta)\rangle$; by the variational principle the result upper-bounds the true ground energy $E_0$.

options.text: an upper bound on the ground-state energy of $H$, by minimizing $\langle H\rangle$ over the ansatz
the exact excited-state spectrum in one shot
the time evolution of a state under $H$
the factorization of a large integer

questions.text: Writing $H=\sum_i w_i P_i$ as a sum of Pauli strings, the energy is:
questions.hint: Expectation is linear, so it distributes over the sum.
questions.explanation: By linearity, $\langle H\rangle=\sum_i w_i\langle P_i\rangle$; each $\langle P_i\rangle$ is measured separately and combined classically.

options.text: $E = \sum_i w_i \langle P_i\rangle$ — a weighted sum of Pauli expectation values
$E = \prod_i \langle P_i\rangle$
$E = \sum_i w_i$ regardless of the state
$E = \langle P_1\rangle$ only

questions.text: How do you measure $\langle X\rangle$ on hardware that only measures in the $Z$ basis?
questions.hint: $H X H = Z$, so a Hadamard maps an $X$ measurement to a $Z$ measurement.
questions.explanation: Since $HXH=Z$, applying $H$ before a $Z$-basis measurement yields $\langle X\rangle$. $Y$ is handled similarly with an $S^\dagger$ then $H$.

options.text: apply a Hadamard first (rotating $X$ into $Z$), then measure in the computational basis
you cannot measure $\langle X\rangle$ at all
measure $\langle Z\rangle$ and negate it
apply a $T$ gate first

questions.hint: Chemistry Hamiltonians map naturally onto qubit Paulis.
questions.explanation: VQE targets electronic-structure problems — molecular ground-state energies — which map onto qubit Hamiltonians and suit shallow near-term circuits.

options.text: estimating molecular ground-state energies in quantum chemistry
sorting large classical databases
breaking RSA encryption
rendering 3D graphics

quizzes.description: MaxCut objective, cost & mixer unitaries, depth, cut values.

questions.hint: It is a near-term optimization heuristic.
questions.explanation: QAOA is a variational heuristic for combinatorial optimization, canonically MaxCut, producing good (not always optimal) solutions.

options.text: approximately solve combinatorial optimization problems such as MaxCut
factor integers exactly
simulate continuous-time dynamics exactly
transmit quantum states over long distances

questions.text: One QAOA layer applies which two unitaries?
questions.hint: Cost phases the basis states; the mixer spreads amplitude.
questions.explanation: Each layer alternates the diagonal cost unitary $e^{-i\gamma C}$ with the transverse-field mixer $e^{-i\beta B}$, $B=\sum_j X_j$; $p$ layers use $2p$ angles.

options.text: a cost unitary $e^{-i\gamma C}$ and a mixer $e^{-i\beta B}$ with $B=\sum_j X_j$
two Hadamard layers
an oracle and a diffusion operator
an inverse QFT and a measurement

questions.text: Increasing the number of QAOA layers $p$ can only improve (never worsen) the best achievable approximation, and as $p\to\infty$ it can reproduce adiabatic evolution.
questions.hint: More layers = a strictly larger variational family.
questions.explanation: True. A depth-$p$ circuit is a special case of depth-$(p{+}1)$, so the optimum is non-decreasing in $p$; the $p\to\infty$ limit can Trotterize adiabatic evolution toward the optimum.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: For MaxCut on a 4-vertex ring (cycle graph $C_4$), what is the maximum number of edges that can be cut?
questions.explanation: $C_4$ is bipartite (colour vertices 0101), so all 4 edges can be cut: the maximum cut is 4.

quizzes.description: Feature maps, quantum kernels, barren plateaus, honest advantage.

questions.text: In a typical quantum machine-learning model, a feature map:
questions.hint: Data is embedded into Hilbert space, then processed variationally.
questions.explanation: A feature map $x\mapsto|\phi(x)\rangle$ embeds classical data into a quantum state; a trainable circuit then acts as a classifier on that embedding.

options.text: encodes a classical data point into a quantum state $|\phi(x)\rangle$, which a variational circuit then classifies
measures the qubits before any gates
is the classical optimizer
removes entanglement from the data

questions.text: A quantum kernel is defined as:
questions.hint: It is the squared overlap of the feature states.
questions.explanation: The quantum kernel is the state overlap $|\langle\phi(x)|\phi(x')\rangle|^2$; if the feature map is classically hard to simulate, the kernel may capture structure classical kernels cannot.

options.text: $K(x,x') = |\langle\phi(x)|\phi(x')\rangle|^2$, a similarity measure that may be hard to compute classically
the number of qubits in the circuit
the classical Euclidean distance between $x$ and $x'$
the depth of the feature-map circuit

questions.explanation: For sufficiently random/expressive deep circuits the gradient concentrates exponentially around zero ($\mathrm{Var}\sim 2^{-n}$), making the landscape essentially flat and the model untrainable.

options.text: for random, deep ansätze the cost gradient's variance vanishes exponentially in the qubit number, so training stalls
the circuit runs out of qubits
the classical optimizer converges too quickly
gradients grow without bound

questions.text: What is the honest status of NISQ variational quantum advantage?
questions.hint: Barren plateaus, noise, and shot cost temper the claims.
questions.explanation: Despite active research, no NISQ variational algorithm has shown a clear practical advantage; barren plateaus, hardware noise, and measurement overhead remain open obstacles.

options.text: no clear, practical quantum advantage has yet been demonstrated; the approaches are promising but unproven
exponential advantage is proven for all machine-learning tasks
variational methods are known to be useless
advantage has been rigorously proven for MaxCut
